paper_title: Pegasos: Primal Estimated sub-GrAdient SOlver for SVM
core_contribution: Pegasos is a stochastic sub-gradient descent algorithm for solving the SVM optimization problem that operates on the primal objective function. It achieves O(1/ε) convergence rate compared to previous O(1/ε²) rates for stochastic methods, with runtime independent of training set size for linear kernels.
algorithm_steps: For each iteration t, randomly select training example (xᵢₜ, yᵢₜ)
If yᵢₜ⟨wₜ, xᵢₜ⟩ < 1, update wₜ₊₁ ← (1-ηₜλ)wₜ + ηₜyᵢₜxᵢₜ
Optional: Project wₜ₊₁ onto ball of radius 1/√λ
key_equations: Eq. (1): SVM primal objective f(w) = λ/2||w||² + (1/m)Σℓ(w;(x,y))
Eq. (6): Projection step wₜ₊₁ ← min{1, 1/√λ/||wₜ₊₁||} wₜ₊₁

key_assumptions.assumption: Training examples are selected uniformly at random at each iteration
key_assumptions.why_needed: Required for the stochastic convergence analysis and unbiased gradient estimation
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When examples are selected in a biased or adversarial order

key_assumptions.why_needed: Ensures strong convexity of the objective function for convergence guarantees
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When λ = 0, the problem becomes non-strongly convex

key_assumptions.assumption: Features are bounded (for linear case runtime analysis)
key_assumptions.why_needed: To bound the number of non-zero features d for runtime complexity
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When dealing with unbounded or very high-dimensional sparse features

baseline_limitations_identified: Previous stochastic gradient methods require O(1/ε²) iterations, decomposition methods have super-linear dependence on training set size, and interior point methods require O(m³) time and O(m²) memory
proposed_improvement: Achieves O(1/ε) iteration complexity through careful step-size selection (ηₜ = 1/(λt)) and operates directly on primal objective with runtime independent of training set size for linear kernels
condition_where_baseline_wins: For very small datasets where the overhead of stochastic sampling is not justified, or when extremely high precision solutions are required where interior point methods' double logarithmic dependence on accuracy becomes advantageous
suitable_toy_datasets: Small text classification datasets like 20newsgroups subset
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets allow testing of the core SVM optimization with clear visualization of convergence behavior and are small enough to compare against exact solutions
reproducibility_notes: Algorithm is clearly specified with explicit step-size formula and update rules. Main implementation challenge is handling the mini-batch variant and optional projection step

key_components_for_ablation.component: Projection step onto ball of radius 1/√λ
key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Constrains the solution to remain within a bounded region
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: May lead to slower convergence but analysis shows similar guarantees without projection

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Ensures proper convergence rate and balances exploration vs exploitation
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Different step-size schedules would likely degrade the O(1/ε) convergence rate

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Controls trade-off between stochastic and batch behavior
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Setting k=1 gives pure stochastic, k=m gives batch gradient descent

known_failure_modes.scenario: When regularization parameter λ is very small
known_failure_modes.reason: Convergence rate depends on 1/λ, so small λ leads to very slow convergence

known_failure_modes.scenario: When data is not randomly shuffled or has strong temporal correlations
known_failure_modes.reason: Violates the random sampling assumption needed for unbiased gradient estimates
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Uniform random selection of training examples
